h1Title: Cancer Pain Management: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment
heroOverview: Cancer pain affects roughly 55% of patients actively receiving cancer treatment and up to 66% of those with advanced or metastatic disease, according to a 2016 Cochrane meta-analysis of 52 studies. It arises from tumour invasion of bone, nerve, or visceral tissue; from cancer treatments including chemotherapy, surgery, and radiation; and from unrelated comorbidities. Pain is the most feared cancer symptom and is undertreated in approximately 43% of patients globally. The WHO three-step analgesic ladder, introduced in 1986 and validated in multiple prospective studies, provides effective pain relief in 70-90% of cancer patients when applied correctly. Modern palliative care integrates opioid titration, adjuvant analgesics, interventional procedures, and psychosocial support to achieve adequate pain control.

keyStats.prevalence: 55% of patients receiving cancer treatment and 66% of advanced-disease patients report pain (Derry et al. Cochrane 2016)
keyStats.demographics: Bone metastases from breast, prostate, and lung cancer are the most common sources of severe cancer pain; neuropathic pain from nerve compression occurs in 30-40% of cancer pain cases
keyStats.avgAge: Median age of cancer diagnosis 66 years; cancer pain burden is highest in advanced-stage disease regardless of age
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 19 million people living with cancer experience pain at any time globally (WHO estimates); 61% of patients with advanced cancer rate their pain as moderate to severe

definition: Cancer pain management (ICD-10: G89.3 — chronic pain associated with neoplasm) is the clinical subspecialty concerned with the assessment, characterisation, and treatment of pain arising in people with a cancer diagnosis. Pain in cancer has multiple mechanisms: nociceptive somatic pain from tumour erosion of bone or soft tissue; nociceptive visceral pain from organ capsule distension, hollow-viscus obstruction, or peritoneal involvement; neuropathic pain from peripheral nerve, plexus, or spinal cord compression or infiltration; and iatrogenic pain from surgery, chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN), radiation fibrosis, or mucositis. These mechanisms frequently coexist in the same patient and require distinct pharmacological strategies. The WHO analgesic ladder classifies treatment intensity into three steps based on pain severity: Step 1 uses non-opioid analgesics (paracetamol, NSAIDs); Step 2 adds mild opioids (codeine, tramadol); Step 3 uses strong opioids (oral morphine, oxycodone, fentanyl, hydromorphone). Adjuvant analgesics — anticonvulsants, antidepressants, corticosteroids, bisphosphonates — are appropriate at any step for specific pain mechanisms. Cancer pain management is provided by palliative care physicians, pain medicine specialists, oncologists, and in many settings by trained primary care practitioners or nurses.

typesClassification.description: Tumour invasion of bone, soft tissue, or integument causes a well-localised, aching or throbbing pain that worsens with movement and responds well to NSAIDs combined with opioids. Bone metastases are the prototypical source, present in up to 70% of breast and prostate cancer patients at autopsy.

Compression or distension of visceral structures — liver capsule from hepatic metastases, bowel from peritoneal disease, bladder from pelvic tumour — produces poorly localised, cramping, or pressure-like pain often referred to distant sites. Opioids with anticholinergic adjuncts address the cramping component.

Direct nerve compression by tumour or injury from chemotherapy, radiation, or surgery causes burning, shooting, or electric pain with allodynia and hyperalgesia. Affects 30-40% of cancer pain patients. Requires adjuvant analgesics (gabapentin, duloxetine) alongside opioids.

Transient exacerbations of pain above the background level, occurring in roughly 65% of cancer patients with controlled baseline pain. Divided into incident pain (triggered by movement or activity) and spontaneous BTcP. Treated with rapid-onset opioids (buccal fentanyl, intranasal fentanyl, or sublingual fentanyl).

Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy, post-surgical pain syndromes, radiation mucositis, and radiation-induced plexopathy are iatrogenic pain conditions that require specialist assessment to distinguish from disease-related pain and to guide adjuvant therapy selection.

primarySymptoms: Constant dull or aching pain at the site of the primary tumour or known metastases, often rated 4-8 on a 0-10 numerical rating scale and present throughout the day.
Breakthrough pain: sudden severe flares lasting 15-30 minutes superimposed on background controlled pain, triggered by coughing, movement, or defaecation, or occurring spontaneously.
Burning, electric, shooting, or stabbing pain in a dermatomal or nerve-territory distribution — hallmarks of neuropathic cancer pain from plexus or root involvement.
Bone pain characterised by deep, relentless ache worsened by weight-bearing, palpation, and position change — typical of vertebral, rib, pelvic, or long-bone metastases.
Visceral pain described as cramping, squeezing, or pressure in the abdomen or pelvis, often without precise localisation, with associated nausea or bloating.
Allodynia (pain from non-painful stimuli such as clothing touch) and hyperalgesia (exaggerated pain to mildly painful stimuli) from central or peripheral sensitisation.
Headache with nausea, vomiting, and visual changes in patients with leptomeningeal or brain metastases — pressure and dural-stretch mechanism.
Pain at rest that disrupts sleep — a clinical indicator of moderate-to-severe pain burden associated with significantly reduced quality of life and depression comorbidity.
Referred shoulder tip or right upper-quadrant pain from hepatic capsule distension by liver metastases — classic visceral referral pattern.
Widespread musculoskeletal fatigue and diffuse aching from immunological and cytokine-driven mechanisms in disseminated disease.
earlyWarningSigns: Increasing analgesic consumption or dose-escalation requests above a previously stable baseline — indicates progressive disease or opioid tolerance developing.
New or changed pain character — a shift from background aching to shooting or burning suggests new neuropathic involvement and warrants reassessment and imaging.
Pain disproportionate to known disease extent — raises concern for pathological fracture, cord compression, or a new metastatic site.
emergencySigns: Sudden severe back or neck pain with bilateral limb weakness, bladder retention, or bowel incontinence — spinal cord compression from vertebral metastases; requires emergency MRI and dexamethasone within 1 hour.
Acute unilateral limb weakness, altered consciousness, or seizure in a patient with known brain metastases — intracranial haemorrhage or rapid oedema; call emergency services immediately.
Severe, acute, uncontrolled pain rated 8-10 unresponsive to rescue doses — requires urgent palliative medicine or emergency department review for parenteral opioid titration.

causes.description: Metastatic lesions in vertebrae, ribs, pelvis, and long bones activate osteoclasts and sensitise periosteal nociceptors through prostaglandin, cytokine, and nerve growth factor release. Bone metastases cause the most prevalent cancer pain syndrome and carry risk of pathological fracture.

causes.cause: Nerve or plexus compression and invasion
causes.description: Tumour infiltration of the brachial plexus (lung, breast), lumbosacral plexus (colorectal, cervical, sarcoma), or individual spinal nerve roots generates neuropathic pain through ectopic discharge from compressed axons and dorsal root ganglion neurons. Neuropathic mechanisms affect 30-40% of cancer pain cases.

Rapidly expanding hepatic metastases stretch the liver capsule; obstructing pancreatic cancer distends bile ducts; peritoneal carcinomatosis compresses bowel. Visceral afferents transmit via sympathetic ganglia and are poorly localised, generating cramping, referred pain, and autonomic features.

Platinum agents (oxaliplatin, cisplatin), taxanes (paclitaxel, docetaxel), and vinca alkaloids cause axonal degeneration in distal sensory and motor fibres, producing burning, tingling, numbness, and allodynia in a glove-and-stocking distribution. Affects 30-68% of patients receiving neurotoxic chemotherapy.

Radiation mucositis (oral and oesophageal), radiation plexopathy, radiation-induced bone necrosis, and pelvic radiation fibrosis are late-effect pain syndromes that develop months to years after treatment and are often progressive and refractory to standard opioids alone.

Post-mastectomy pain syndrome, post-thoracotomy pain, and phantom limb pain after limb amputation affect 20-60% of cancer surgery patients. Intercostal nerve injury and scar neuroma formation sustain neuropathic mechanisms long after wound healing.

riskFactors.description: Pain prevalence rises from roughly 33% in early-stage disease to 66% in advanced cancer. Bone and visceral metastases are the primary drivers of escalating pain burden.
Bone and lung cancer produce the highest pain intensity scores; pancreatic and cervical cancers are also associated with severe pain in approximately 70-80% of patients with advanced disease.

riskFactors.factor: Prior opioid exposure or opioid-use disorder
riskFactors.description: Pre-existing opioid tolerance requires higher starting doses; active substance-use disorder complicates monitoring but is not a contraindication to opioid analgesics with appropriate safeguards.
Psychological distress amplifies pain perception through central sensitisation and fear-avoidance; patients with depression rate pain 1-2 points higher on NRS even at equivalent tissue-injury levels.
Cumulative lifetime dose of platinum agents and taxanes directly predicts CIPN severity; dose reduction is the primary preventive strategy but may compromise oncological outcomes.

riskFactors.factor: Inadequate pain assessment and undertreated pain
riskFactors.description: WHO estimates 43% of cancer patients globally receive inadequate analgesia. Under-prescribing of opioids due to regulatory barriers, opiophobia, and poor assessment perpetuates unnecessary suffering.

affectedDemographics: Pain is equally prevalent across sexes but women report higher pain intensity scores in some cancer types, potentially reflecting reporting differences and differential opioid metabolism.

Older adults (>65) face barriers to adequate opioid analgesia from polypharmacy, renal impairment that requires dose adjustment of morphine metabolites, and cognitive impairment limiting self-reporting.

Low- and middle-income countries bear a disproportionate pain burden: morphine availability is critically limited in 80% of LMICs despite accounting for roughly 60% of global cancer burden (WHO IARC 2022).
Children with cancer are systematically under-assessed and undertreated for pain due to inadequate paediatric palliative care infrastructure in most countries.
diagnosisOverview: Effective cancer pain management starts with systematic, validated assessment. The Numerical Rating Scale (NRS 0-10) is the standard one-dimensional tool; the Brief Pain Inventory (BPI) captures pain intensity, location, and functional interference. Assessment must characterise the pain mechanism — somatic, visceral, or neuropathic — because this determines adjuvant analgesic selection. The clinician documents pain at rest and on movement, pain character (aching, burning, stabbing, cramping), temporal pattern (constant versus incident versus spontaneous breakthrough), and associated features (allodynia, referred pain). SOCRATES mnemonic captures the core elements: Site, Onset, Character, Radiation, Alleviating factors, Timing, Exacerbating factors, Severity. Imaging guides treatment planning: CT identifies bone metastases and neural foraminal compromise; PET-CT maps metastatic burden; MRI is mandatory when cord compression is suspected. The revised Edmonton Symptom Assessment Scale (ESAS-r) provides a broader symptom burden context including pain, fatigue, nausea, anxiety, and wellbeing. Specialist palliative care or pain medicine assessment should be sought when pain is rated ≥7 on NRS, when multiple mechanisms coexist, when opioid dose escalation is rapid, or when interventional approaches are being considered.

diagnosticTests.test: Numerical Rating Scale (NRS) and Brief Pain Inventory (BPI)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Standardised assessment of pain intensity, location, quality, and functional interference at each clinical encounter
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient-completed questionnaires taking 2-5 minutes. The BPI includes a body diagram to map pain locations. Results guide treatment titration and are recorded in the medical record to track trends.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies bone metastases, soft-tissue masses, visceral involvement, and neural foraminal compromise
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 10-20 minute scan. Contrast dye may be injected via a hand vein. Radiation exposure equivalent to several chest X-rays. Results reviewed by radiologist within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Mandatory for suspected spinal cord or nerve root compression by epidural metastases
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes lying still in the scanner. Contrast injected via hand vein. No radiation. Results reviewed urgently when cord compression is suspected — within 1-2 hours at a cancer centre.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps the full extent of bone metastases and identifies pain sources not visible on plain X-ray
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bone scan requires a radiotracer injection 2-3 hours before a 30-minute scan. PET-CT takes 60-90 minutes and uses a short-lived radiotracer (FDG) injected intravenously.

diagnosticTests.test: Nerve conduction studies and quantitative sensory testing
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy severity and distinguishes from tumour-related neuropathic pain
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 60-90 minutes in a neurophysiology lab. Mild electrical stimuli applied to skin; needle electrodes in selected muscles. Identifies axonal versus demyelinating pattern and severity.

treatmentOverview: The WHO analgesic ladder remains the globally endorsed framework, providing effective relief in 70-90% of patients when applied correctly. Step 1 (mild pain): paracetamol 1 g four times daily and/or an NSAID (ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily, celecoxib 200 mg twice daily). Step 2 (moderate pain): add a weak opioid — codeine 30-60 mg every 4-6 hours or tramadol 50-100 mg every 6 hours. Step 3 (severe pain): replace with a strong opioid. Oral morphine is the WHO recommended first-choice strong opioid; the starting dose is 5-10 mg every 4 hours with an equal rescue dose available every hour for breakthrough pain. Once stable, convert to modified-release morphine (MST 12-hourly) with short-acting morphine for BTcP. Opioid rotation is used when dose escalation produces intolerable side-effects: oxycodone, hydromorphone, fentanyl patch, or buprenorphine patch are alternatives. Equianalgesic conversion tables guide rotation. A concurrent laxative (macrogol, senna) is mandatory with every opioid; anti-emetics (metoclopramide, haloperidol) for the first 1-2 weeks; and naloxone (Targinact formulation) where constipation is refractory. Adjuvant analgesics added to the opioid backbone for specific mechanisms include: dexamethasone for bone pain and peritumoural oedema; gabapentin or pregabalin for neuropathic pain; duloxetine for CIPN; bisphosphonates or denosumab for bone-metastasis pain; and ketamine infusion for refractory or opioid-resistant pain. Interventional procedures — epidural or intrathecal drug delivery, neurolytic coeliac plexus block, vertebroplasty, kyphoplasty — are considered when systemic opioids are insufficient or cause limiting side-effects.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral morphine (5-10 mg every 4 hours, titrated as needed)
medicalTreatments.description: WHO first-choice strong opioid for moderate-to-severe cancer pain. Start at a low dose and titrate in 25-33% increments every 24-48 hours based on rescue-dose consumption. Convert to modified-release formulation once stable. Prescribe laxative concurrently.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adequate pain control in roughly 80% of cancer patients when properly titrated per WHO guidance (Ventafridda et al. 1987, WHO 2019)

medicalTreatments.name: Dexamethasone (4-16 mg/day oral or IV)
medicalTreatments.description: Potent corticosteroid that reduces peritumoural oedema, suppresses prostaglandin-mediated bone pain, and improves appetite and mood. Particularly effective for bone metastasis pain, liver capsule pain, and spinal cord compression. Use shortest effective course to limit steroid side-effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain reduction of 1-2 NRS points within 48-72 hours in 60-70% of patients with bone or visceral metastasis pain

medicalTreatments.name: Gabapentin (300-3,600 mg/day in divided doses) or pregabalin (75-300 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line adjuvant analgesics for neuropathic cancer pain from nerve compression or CIPN. Reduce ectopic nerve discharge and central sensitisation. Titrate slowly from low dose to avoid sedation and dizziness.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NRS reduction of 1.5-2 points versus placebo in neuropathic cancer pain RCTs; response in 50-60% of patients

medicalTreatments.name: Zoledronic acid (4 mg IV every 3-4 weeks) or denosumab (120 mg SC monthly)
medicalTreatments.description: Bone-targeted agents for skeletal metastases from breast, prostate, lung, and other solid tumours. Reduce skeletal-related events (fractures, cord compression, hypercalcaemia) and provide modest direct analgesic benefit through osteoclast inhibition.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduction in skeletal-related events by 25-35% in phase-3 trials; pain scores improved by 1-1.5 NRS points in bone-metastasis pain cohorts

medicalTreatments.name: Transmucosal fentanyl (buccal, sublingual, or intranasal)
medicalTreatments.description: Rapid-onset opioid formulations for breakthrough cancer pain. Onset 5-15 minutes versus 30-60 minutes for oral morphine. Dose is titrated independently from background opioid — effective BTcP dose does not directly correlate with the background opioid dose.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Statistically superior to oral morphine rescue for BTcP at 15 and 30 minutes in head-to-head RCTs; NRS improvement 2-3 points at 30 minutes

medicalTreatments.name: Intrathecal drug delivery (spinal opioid ± local anaesthetic ± ziconotide)
medicalTreatments.description: Implanted pump delivering morphine or hydromorphone directly into the intrathecal space at 1/100th the systemic dose required. Reserved for refractory pain or intolerable systemic opioid side-effects. Requires specialist implantation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinically Meaningful Pain relief and significant reduction in systemic opioid dose in 60-80% of carefully selected patients; IDDS Trial 2002 showed superior outcomes vs continued medical management

surgicalOptions.name: Vertebroplasty or kyphoplasty
surgicalOptions.description: Percutaneous injection of bone cement into a fractured or collapsing vertebral body from metastatic disease. Kyphoplasty uses a balloon to restore vertebral height before cement injection. Performed under imaging guidance under sedation or general anaesthesia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pain relief in 70-90% of patients within 24-48 hours of the procedure; durability approximately 6-12 months in metastatic bone disease

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical spinal decompression and stabilisation
surgicalOptions.description: Laminectomy with or without posterior spinal fusion for epidural spinal cord compression causing progressive neurological deficit. Best outcomes when performed within 24 hours of cord compression onset.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Ambulation preserved or restored in 75-85% of patients when operated within 24 hours of diagnosis of cord compression

alternativeTreatments.name: Neurolytic coeliac plexus block
alternativeTreatments.description: Ethanol or phenol injection into the coeliac plexus under CT or EUS guidance, ablating visceral afferents from the upper abdomen. RCTs and meta-analyses support its use in pancreatic cancer pain — reduces opioid requirements by 30-40% and improves pain scores versus systemic opioids alone.

alternativeTreatments.name: Palliative radiotherapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Single-fraction or short-course external beam radiotherapy to painful bone metastases achieves pain relief in approximately 60-80% of patients within 4-8 weeks. Reirradiation is feasible. Endorsed by ASTRO 2017 guidelines as first-line for uncomplicated painful bone metastases.

whySeeSpecialist: Specialist palliative care or pain medicine review is indicated when pain is rated ≥7/10, when dose escalation is occurring more frequently than every 48-72 hours, when neuropathic features are prominent, or when opioid side-effects are limiting function. Interventional options (intrathecal pump, nerve blocks) require specialist assessment at a pain medicine or anaesthesia centre.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a palliative care physician or pain medicine specialist with documented cancer pain management experience, ideally within or affiliated with a comprehensive cancer centre. For interventional procedures, verify the physician's training in image-guided nerve blocks and intraspinal drug delivery. An interdisciplinary team including specialist nurses, pharmacists, and psychologists produces better outcomes than a single-clinician model.
hospitalCriteria: Access to the full WHO analgesic ladder including strong opioids without administrative delay
Palliative care consultation service available seven days per week
Interventional pain service for nerve blocks, vertebroplasty, and intrathecal device implantation
Radiation oncology service for palliative bone radiotherapy
Specialist palliative care nursing and pharmacy support for opioid rotation and titration
keyFacilities: Comprehensive cancer centres with integrated palliative care
Inpatient palliative care units for complex refractory pain
Outpatient pain management clinics within oncology departments
Hospice services for end-of-life pain control
preventionStrategies: Begin analgesic therapy early — treat pain rated ≥4/10 before it escalates to severe, as undertreated acute pain promotes central sensitisation.
Use neurotoxic chemotherapy at the lowest effective dose when possible; dose reduction or substitution reduces CIPN incidence without compromising all cancer outcomes.
Prescribe a concurrent laxative with every opioid to prevent constipation — the most universal and predictable opioid side-effect.
Screen for pain at every oncology visit using a validated scale (NRS or BPI) — systematic screening reduces undertreatment rates.
Bisphosphonate or denosumab therapy in patients with bone metastases reduces skeletal-related events and subsequent pain crises.
Radiation therapy to symptomatic bone metastases before pathological fracture reduces pain intensity and prevents more complex complications.
lifestyleModifications: Take scheduled opioids at regular fixed intervals rather than waiting for pain to become severe — maintaining steady plasma levels provides more stable control.
Keep a pain diary recording intensity, location, character, and rescue-dose use to support accurate titration at clinical visits.
Apply heat packs to areas of bone or muscle pain for temporary symptomatic relief between doses.
Gentle movement and physiotherapy maintain joint mobility and reduce pain related to immobility, unless movement is contraindicated by fracture risk.
Manage anxiety and catastrophising through cancer psychology support — psychological distress amplifies pain perception by 1-2 NRS points independent of disease burden.
Ensure adequate sleep; poor sleep lowers pain threshold and reduces analgesic efficacy.

recommended: High-fibre foods (whole grains, fruits, vegetables) to counter opioid-induced constipation when tolerated
Adequate hydration (1.5-2 L/day) to support renal clearance of opioid metabolites and reduce constipation
Small frequent meals when nausea is present from opioids or disease
Protein-rich foods (lean meat, eggs, legumes) to maintain muscle mass during cancer treatment
avoid: Alcohol during opioid therapy — synergistic CNS depression increases sedation and respiratory depression risk
Grapefruit and St John's Wort when taking fentanyl or methadone — CYP3A4 inhibition raises opioid blood levels unpredictably
High caffeine intake during opioid titration as it can worsen insomnia and amplify pain sensitisation

exerciseGuidelines: Gentle walking, stretching, and physiotherapy-guided range-of-motion exercises are appropriate for most cancer pain patients. Axial loading and impact exercise are contraindicated when vertebral or long-bone metastases carry fracture risk — DEXA and oncologist guidance required. Aquatic exercise reduces gravitational load and is well tolerated. Physiotherapy should be integrated into the pain management plan, particularly for CIPN and post-surgical pain syndromes.
dailyManagement: Take long-acting opioids at fixed times every 12 or 24 hours regardless of pain level to maintain steady analgesic plasma concentration.
Use rescue (breakthrough) opioid immediately at onset of BTcP — early intervention reduces episode duration and prevents pain escalation.
Take laxative daily — do not wait for constipation to develop; the goal is one soft bowel motion every 1-2 days.
Report new or changed pain character promptly — a shift from background to neuropathic features may indicate disease progression requiring imaging.
Attend scheduled oncology and palliative care reviews to allow timely dose adjustment before tolerance or disease progression causes uncontrolled pain.
prognosis: With adequate analgesic treatment following the WHO ladder, approximately 70-90% of cancer patients achieve acceptable pain control (NRS ≤4) for most of their illness. The remaining 10-30% have complex refractory pain requiring specialist palliative care input and often interventional procedures. Pain control does not shorten survival; palliative care integrated early improves survival by 2-3 months in patients with advanced non-small-cell lung cancer (Temel et al. NEJM 2010). Patients who receive specialist palliative care early have better pain scores, improved quality of life, and reduced emergency hospital admissions. Poor prognostic factors for pain control include neuropathic pain mechanisms, rapid disease progression, psychological distress, cognitive impairment, and opioid addiction history. End-of-life pain — even in the final hours — is controllable with appropriate opioid and sedation protocols in specialist palliative care units.
recoveryTimeline: Pain relief from opioid initiation: hours to 2 days for titration to effect. Bone pain from palliative radiotherapy: response within 1-4 weeks, peak effect at 4-8 weeks. CIPN improvement after chemotherapy completion: partial recovery in 50-60% of patients over 3-12 months; complete recovery uncommon with severe CIPN. Intrathecal pump benefit: rapid improvement within days of dose optimisation.
complications: Opioid-induced constipation in virtually all patients (up to 90%) — prevents adequate analgesic dose escalation if untreated; managed proactively with macrogol and stimulant laxatives.
Opioid-induced neurotoxicity (myoclonus, delirium, hallucinations, hyperalgesia) at high doses or in renal impairment — treated by opioid rotation, dose reduction, and hydration.
Spinal cord compression from vertebral metastases — causes permanent paraplegia if not treated within 24-48 hours; requires emergency MRI and dexamethasone.
Pathological fracture of long bones or vertebrae from untreated bone metastases — preventable with bisphosphonates, denosumab, and local radiotherapy.
Under-treatment of pain leading to central sensitisation, psychological trauma, loss of function, and avoidable hospitalisation in 43% of patients globally.
Respiratory depression from opioid overdose — rare with correct titration but reversed rapidly with naloxone 400 mcg IV or IM.

supportResources.name: WHO Guidelines for the Pharmacological and Radiotherapeutic Management of Cancer Pain in Adults and Adolescents
supportResources.description: Official WHO clinical guideline providing the analgesic ladder and evidence-based treatment recommendations.

supportResources.name: Palliative Care Australia
supportResources.description: National peak body for palliative care with patient resources, care finders, and pain management guides.

supportResources.name: American Cancer Society — Managing Cancer Pain
supportResources.description: Patient-facing resource on cancer pain types, medication options, and communication with the care team.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic pain syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic pain syndrome is a biopsychosocial diagnosis in patients without active tissue injury driving the pain. Cancer pain has a specific ongoing nociceptive or neuropathic mechanism linked to tumour or treatment; the management priority is adequate analgesic titration rather than psychological pain-management programmes, though psychological support is complementary.

confusedWithConditions.name: Neuropathic pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Neuropathic pain is a mechanism, not a diagnosis. Cancer-related neuropathic pain from plexus invasion shares features with non-cancer neuropathic pain but is managed in the context of progressive disease, concurrent opioids, and often more aggressive dose titration. CIPN is a specific treatment-related neuropathic syndrome.

confusedWithConditions.name: Opioid-induced hyperalgesia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Opioid-induced hyperalgesia (OIH) is a paradoxical increase in pain sensitivity from long-term opioid exposure. In cancer pain management it manifests as escalating pain without disease progression. Distinguished by worsening pain on dose increase and diffuse allodynia; managed by opioid dose reduction or rotation rather than escalation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Breakthrough pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Breakthrough pain specifically refers to transient pain exacerbations above controlled background pain; it is a subtype of cancer pain rather than a separate condition. It requires rapid-onset opioid rescue medication and a review of background analgesic adequacy.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic pain syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Overlapping pain-management principles; cancer pain shares central sensitisation mechanisms with chronic non-cancer pain.

relatedConditions.name: Breakthrough pain
relatedConditions.relevance: A distinct pain subtype occurring in 65% of cancer patients on background opioids, requiring separate rapid-onset analgesic management.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Nerve invasion and CIPN generate neuropathic mechanisms in 30-40% of cancer pain cases, requiring adjuvant analgesics beyond standard opioids.

relatedConditions.name: Intractable pain
relatedConditions.relevance: A proportion of cancer pain patients develop opioid-refractory pain requiring escalation to interventional pain management and specialist input.

relatedConditions.name: Lung cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Lung cancer is one of the most common sources of severe cancer pain through bone metastases, Pancoast syndrome, and brachial plexus invasion.

faqs.question: What is the WHO analgesic ladder for cancer pain?
faqs.answer: The WHO analgesic ladder is a three-step framework. Step 1 uses non-opioids (paracetamol, NSAIDs) for mild pain. Step 2 adds weak opioids (codeine, tramadol) for moderate pain. Step 3 uses strong opioids (morphine, oxycodone, fentanyl) for severe pain. Adjuvant analgesics are added at any step for specific pain types.

faqs.question: Is morphine the best drug for cancer pain?
faqs.answer: Oral morphine is the WHO-recommended first-choice strong opioid for moderate-to-severe cancer pain. It is low-cost, widely available, and has a long safety record. Alternative strong opioids — oxycodone, hydromorphone, fentanyl — are used when morphine causes intolerable side-effects or when renal impairment requires avoiding active metabolite accumulation.

faqs.question: What percentage of cancer patients experience pain?
faqs.answer: Approximately 55% of patients actively receiving cancer treatment and 66% of those with advanced or metastatic disease report pain. Among patients with any stage of disease at any point in their illness, roughly 38-50% experience clinically significant pain.

faqs.question: Does using opioids for cancer pain lead to addiction?
faqs.answer: Addiction is rare when opioids are used for genuine pain in cancer patients. Physical dependence (need for gradual dose reduction on cessation) is expected but is not addiction. Less than 1% of cancer patients develop opioid addiction. Fear of addiction should not prevent adequate prescribing of opioids for cancer pain.

faqs.question: What is breakthrough cancer pain?
faqs.answer: Breakthrough cancer pain (BTcP) is a sudden, brief flare of pain above a controlled background level, lasting typically 15-30 minutes. It occurs in roughly 65% of cancer patients with stable background pain. It is treated with rapid-onset opioids — buccal fentanyl, sublingual fentanyl, or intranasal fentanyl — which onset within 5-15 minutes.

faqs.question: Can cancer pain be fully controlled?
faqs.answer: Adequate pain control — defined as pain rated 4 or less on a 0-10 scale — is achieved in 70-90% of patients when the WHO analgesic ladder is correctly applied. Refractory pain in the remaining 10-30% requires specialist palliative care, opioid rotation, or interventional procedures such as intrathecal drug delivery.

faqs.question: What are adjuvant analgesics in cancer pain?
faqs.answer: Adjuvant analgesics are drugs whose primary indication is not pain but which provide pain relief for specific mechanisms. Gabapentin and pregabalin target neuropathic nerve pain. Dexamethasone reduces bone and visceral pain via anti-inflammatory action. Bisphosphonates and denosumab treat bone metastasis pain. Duloxetine is first-line for chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy.

faqs.question: Does cancer pain mean the disease is getting worse?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Pain can arise from treatment side-effects, post-surgical syndromes, or unrelated conditions rather than tumour progression. However, new or changing pain character always warrants clinical assessment and often imaging to exclude disease progression.

faqs.question: What is the difference between palliative care and cancer pain management?
faqs.answer: Cancer pain management is a component of palliative care but is also provided by oncologists, GPs, and pain specialists. Palliative care is a broader specialty addressing physical symptoms, psychological distress, social needs, and spiritual care throughout illness. Cancer pain management focuses specifically on analgesic assessment and treatment.

faqs.question: What is neuropathic cancer pain?
faqs.answer: Neuropathic cancer pain arises when tumour invades or compresses nerves, or when chemotherapy damages peripheral nerves. It presents as burning, shooting, or electric sensations with allodynia and hyperalgesia. It affects 30-40% of cancer pain patients and requires adjuvant analgesics such as gabapentin alongside standard opioids.

faqs.question: Can radiotherapy treat cancer pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Palliative radiotherapy to painful bone metastases achieves pain relief in approximately 60-80% of patients within 4-8 weeks. A single 8 Gy fraction is as effective as longer courses for uncomplicated bone metastasis pain. It is endorsed by ASTRO 2017 as first-line treatment for painful bone metastases.

faqs.question: What is chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN)?
faqs.answer: CIPN is nerve damage caused by platinum drugs, taxanes, and vinca alkaloids, producing burning, tingling, and numbness in a glove-and-stocking distribution. It affects 30-68% of patients receiving neurotoxic chemotherapy. Duloxetine is the only agent with RCT evidence for CIPN pain. Dose reduction is the primary preventive strategy.

faqs.question: How is cancer bone pain treated?
faqs.answer: Cancer bone pain is treated with NSAIDs and opioids as the analgesic backbone, combined with dexamethasone for inflammatory oedema, bisphosphonates or denosumab for osteoclast inhibition, and palliative radiotherapy to the painful metastatic site. Vertebroplasty or kyphoplasty treats pain from vertebral compression fractures.

faqs.question: What is an intrathecal drug delivery system for cancer pain?
faqs.answer: An intrathecal pump delivers opioid directly into the spinal fluid at 1/100th the systemic dose, reducing side-effects while achieving superior pain control. It is indicated for patients with refractory cancer pain or intolerable systemic opioid effects. Improvement occurs in 60-80% of carefully selected candidates.

faqs.question: When should palliative care be involved in cancer pain management?
faqs.answer: Early palliative care involvement — at diagnosis of advanced cancer rather than only at end of life — improves pain scores, quality of life, and in one landmark trial extended survival by 2.7 months. Specialist referral is indicated when pain is uncontrolled, opioid titration is complex, or psychological distress amplifies pain burden.

metaTitle: Cancer Pain Management: Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Cancer pain affects 55-66% of patients. WHO ladder opioids, adjuvant analgesics, radiotherapy, and nerve blocks reviewed. Palliative care options explained.
